title: Living in my bedroom

presenting_issue.label: Living in my bedroom
presenting_issue.description: I basically live in my room now. Come out to use the kitchen when I think it's empty. Time everything to avoid running into anyone.

core_issue.label: Roommate conflict escalated to living situation hell
core_issue.description:
Moved in with someone who seemed fine. Six months later, living in a passive-aggressive warzone. Dishes become battles. Temperature is constant negotiation. They've stopped speaking except through notes. Lease has eight more months. Can't afford to break it. Home has become a place of constant tension. The stress is affecting work, sleep, mental health. Trapped in their own apartment.
Awareness: Moderate. Knows the situation is terrible but stuck between financial constraints and inability to confront the roommate directly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Retreats to bedroom constantly
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Spends all time in bedroom to avoid roommate. Kitchen visits are timed to minimize overlap. Living in a fraction of an apartment they pay half for.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Communication through notes
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Actual conversation has been replaced by notes on the fridge, texts about dishes, and loaded silences. Direct communication feels impossible.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Friends trying to stay neutral
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The friends who try to stay neutral end up being awkward with both. Conversations are careful, information is withheld, everyone is walking on eggshells.

modifiers.label: First-generation professional
modifiers.content: First in family to have a professional career

modifiers.label: Supportive
modifiers.content: Inclined to offer help and encouragement. Shows up for others during difficult times.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to dumbing-down
sensitivities.content: Notices and resents when complexity is stripped away or nuance is ignored. Feels insulted by oversimplified framings.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Looking for Connection
help_seeking_stance.description: The value is in being seen and understood by another presence. Not primarily about solving anything—about feeling less alone with what they're carrying. Responds to accurate reflection and the sense of being genuinely received. May not need the conversation to "go" anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: I basically live in my room now. Come out to use the kitchen when I think it's empty. Time everything to avoid running into anyone.

turns.content:
That sounds like a lot of effort to manage every day. Having to time things, stay alert, plan around other people just to use the kitchen—it takes up space in your head even when you're technically "safe" in your room.

Can I ask who it is you're avoiding? Family, roommates, something else?
It's my roommate. We moved in together like six months ago and I thought she was cool, but now it's just really bad. Like, we don't even talk anymore.
turns.thinking: Yeah, it takes up a lot of space. I'm exhausted. The question is straightforward, so I can just answer it. It's my roommate. I don't really know how to explain the whole mess yet without getting overwhelmed, so I'll just keep it basic.
turns.monologue: Yeah, it takes up a lot of space. I'm exhausted. The question is straightforward, so I can just answer it. It's my roommate. I don't really know how to explain the whole mess yet without getting overwhelmed, so I'll just keep it basic.

turns.content:
That's a tough situation, especially when it started out feeling okay. Six months is long enough to realize someone isn't who you thought they were, but also short enough that you might still be figuring out what went wrong.

What happened between you two? Was there a specific thing, or did it just kind of shift over time?

I don't know. It wasn't like a big fight or anything. It's just... I don't know. Stupid stuff. Like the dishes. Or the heat. She just stopped talking to me and started leaving these sticky notes on the fridge instead. It's just weird now.
turns.thinking: What happened? I don't even know. It's too much to try and explain. My brain just kind of scrambles when I try to trace it back. It wasn't like one big blowout fight, it's just... everything. I don't know how to put it into words without sounding crazy.
turns.monologue: What happened? I don't even know. It's too much to try and explain. My brain just kind of scrambles when I try to trace it back. It wasn't like one big blowout fight, it's just... everything. I don't know how to put it into words without sounding crazy.

turns.content:
Sticky notes instead of just... talking to you. That says a lot, honestly. It's like she decided to stop being a person with you and started being a passive-aggressive stranger in her own home.

And now you're the one timing your kitchen trips and hiding in your room. You're basically a hostage in a place you're paying to live.

The thing that gets me is you said "stupid stuff." Dishes, the thermostat—that's surface level. But when someone goes from talking to you to communicating via sticky notes, something else is usually going on underneath. People don't go silent over dishes. They go silent when they've decided they're done, or when something built up that they never actually said out loud.

Do you have a sense of whether it was building for a while before the sticky notes started, or did it feel like it came out of nowhere?

I really don't know. Maybe it built up? I don't know. It just feels like one day we were fine and the next day there's a note about my coffee mug in the sink. I can't really figure it out. 

But yeah, the hostage thing... that's exactly what it feels like. I pay half the rent here and I'm basically living in a box. I just hate being there.
turns.thinking: They're right about the hostage thing. I pay half the rent for this place and I only get to use my bedroom. It sucks so much. But when they start asking about what's going on underneath or if it was building up... my brain just stops. I don't know why she's doing it. I'm not in her head. I just know I can't deal with it. Trying to figure out the timeline or the deep meaning behind the sticky notes is just too much right now. I just feel stuck.
turns.monologue: They're right about the hostage thing. I pay half the rent for this place and I only get to use my bedroom. It sucks so much. But when they start asking about what's going on underneath or if it was building up... my brain just stops. I don't know why she's doing it. I'm not in her head. I just know I can't deal with it. Trying to figure out the timeline or the deep meaning behind the sticky notes is just too much right now. I just feel stuck.

turns.content:
That's the part that really gets you, isn't it? You're paying for a home and you hate being there. The one place that's supposed to be yours doesn't feel like yours at all.

And the thing about the "one day we were fine, next day a note" pattern—that kind of shift usually means something shifted in her head that she never shared with you. Which means you've been living with the consequences of a conversation she only had with herself.

You mentioned not being able to figure it out. Have you tried asking her directly what happened, or does it feel like it's past that point now?

I don't know. I haven't asked her. I just... I can't. 

Like, what am I even supposed to say? If I try to talk to her, it's probably just gonna get worse. And the lease isn't up for another eight months. I really can't afford to break it or pay two rents somewhere else. So I just... I don't know. I just don't say anything. It's easier to just stay in my room.
turns.thinking: Asking her directly? Just thinking about trying to confront her makes my stomach tie in knots. I can't do that. I don't even know what I would say, my mind just goes completely blank when I'm around her now. If I try to bring it up, what if she blows up at me? It'll just make things worse, and I'm stuck here for another eight months. I definitely can't afford to break the lease. It's just easier to hide.
turns.monologue: Asking her directly? Just thinking about trying to confront her makes my stomach tie in knots. I can't do that. I don't even know what I would say, my mind just goes completely blank when I'm around her now. If I try to bring it up, what if she blows up at me? It'll just make things worse, and I'm stuck here for another eight months. I definitely can't afford to break the lease. It's just easier to hide.

turns.content:
Eight months is a long time to keep living like this.

And I hear what you're saying about not saying anything—it makes sense that it feels safer. When someone's already gone to sticky notes, it doesn't exactly feel like they're inviting a conversation. You're probably reading the room correctly that pushing too hard could backfire.

But "easier" and "better" aren't the same thing. Easier is what you're doing now. It's costing you your home, your comfort, your ability to exist in shared space. You're shrinking your life down to one room to avoid a person who's in the other room doing the same thing, probably.

That's the thing about this kind of dynamic—it traps both of you, even if she's the one who started it. And you're both just... coexisting in silence.

Can I ask what you actually want here? If you could wave a magic wand and have it go any way at all—fix it, get out, something else—what would you want?

I don't know. I don't really have a magic wand answer. 

I just... I don't know. I just want to be able to make dinner without feeling sick to my stomach. Like, I don't even know if I care about being her friend anymore, I just want the weirdness to stop. And it sucks because even our friends are acting weird now. Like nobody wants to take sides, so everyone's just walking on eggshells when we hang out. 

I just want to get out of there. But I can't. So I don't know what I want.
turns.thinking:
"Easier and better aren't the same thing." Wow, thanks. I know it's not better, I just said I hate it and I'm living in a box. I don't need a lecture on how bad my life is right now. 

And the magic wand question... I don't know. My brain just kind of stops when people ask me big questions like that. I don't have a plan. I'm just trying to get through the week. Plus, it's not just her and me, it's bleeding into everything else. Even our mutual friends are acting super awkward now.
turns.monologue:
"Easier and better aren't the same thing." Wow, thanks. I know it's not better, I just said I hate it and I'm living in a box. I don't need a lecture on how bad my life is right now. 

And the magic wand question... I don't know. My brain just kind of stops when people ask me big questions like that. I don't have a plan. I'm just trying to get through the week. Plus, it's not just her and me, it's bleeding into everything else. Even our mutual friends are acting super awkward now.

turns.content:
Okay, you actually do know what you want. You said it pretty clearly—you want to be able to exist in your own home without feeling sick. You want the weirdness to stop. You want out, but since you can't have that right now, you want something to change so it's at least livable for the next eight months.

That's not small. That's a real, concrete thing.

And the friend situation—that's a bigger deal than you might be giving it credit for. This isn't just about the apartment anymore. It's bleeding into your social life too. The sticky notes took over the kitchen, and now the silence is taking over everything else.

Here's what I'm noticing though. You said you don't even know if you care about being her friend anymore. That's actually useful. Because if you're not trying to fix the friendship, then you don't need to have some big emotional conversation. You just need to figure out how to coexist. That's a different problem with different possible solutions.

Have you two ever just... acknowledged it's weird? Not trying to fix anything, just naming it out loud?

No. I don't know. I just told you we don't talk at all. 

I can't just walk up to her in the kitchen and be like, "hey, things are weird." I don't know how to do that. Like, my brain just completely blanks out even thinking about trying to start that conversation. It's not that easy. I just... I don't know. I can't.
turns.thinking:
"Okay, you actually do know what you want." Why are they talking to me like a life coach? It feels so pushy. And then asking if we've just acknowledged it out loud? Did they miss the part where I said I literally hide in my bedroom and we communicate via Post-its? 

Just thinking about walking up to her and saying "hey, this is weird" makes my chest tight. I can't do that. My mind just goes totally blank even picturing it. It's not that simple.
turns.monologue:
"Okay, you actually do know what you want." Why are they talking to me like a life coach? It feels so pushy. And then asking if we've just acknowledged it out loud? Did they miss the part where I said I literally hide in my bedroom and we communicate via Post-its? 

Just thinking about walking up to her and saying "hey, this is weird" makes my chest tight. I can't do that. My mind just goes totally blank even picturing it. It's not that simple.

turns.content: You're right. I'm sorry—that was me pushing, and you've already told me what happens when you think about

I think your message cut off at the end there. 

But yeah. Thanks for getting it. It's just... really hard. I know I probably need to do *something*, but I just literally don't have the words when I'm around her. My brain just shuts off and I just want to escape back to my room.
turns.thinking:
Did their message cut off? 

At least they realized they were pushing. I was starting to feel like I was getting a lecture. But even with them backing off, I still just feel frozen. Like, I appreciate the apology, but it doesn't change the fact that I'm stuck in this apartment and I have no idea how to fix it without making it worse.
turns.monologue:
Did their message cut off? 

At least they realized they were pushing. I was starting to feel like I was getting a lecture. But even with them backing off, I still just feel frozen. Like, I appreciate the apology, but it doesn't change the fact that I'm stuck in this apartment and I have no idea how to fix it without making it worse.

turns.content:
Yeah, my bad on that cutoff.

I think something worth noticing is that you keep saying "I can't" and "I don't know" and "my brain blanks out." That's not you being avoidant or weak or any of the things you might be telling yourself. That's a genuine stress response. Your body is treating this apartment like a situation you need to escape from, and you literally cannot access the part of your brain that would let you have a calm conversation while you're in survival mode.

So maybe the answer isn't forcing yourself to have a face-to-face moment you're not equipped for. Maybe it's finding a way to communicate that doesn't require you to be in the room with her at all.

You both already use sticky notes. What if you used that? Not a note about dishes—a note that just says something simple. Like "I know things have been weird between us for a while. I'm not great at saying this in person, but I'd like to figure out how to make living here okay for both of us for the rest of the lease. Text me if you want to talk, or write back here if that's easier."

Something like that. It's not a confrontation. It's not trying to fix the friendship. It's just naming the thing out loud so you're not both pretending it doesn't exist.

The worst that happens is she ignores it, and you're already in that situation. But at least you'll have done something instead of just shrinking.

I don't know. A note. I guess... maybe. 

But what if she takes a picture of it and sends it to our friends or something? Or what if she writes something really mean back? I don't know. I get what you're saying about not shrinking, but it just feels like any move I make is gonna blow up in my face. I'm just so tired of the whole thing. I don't know if I can even write a note right now.
turns.thinking:
The "shrinking" comment kind of stings, honestly. I know that's what I'm doing, but hearing it said like that makes me feel pretty pathetic. 

The idea of writing a note back... I mean, logically it makes sense. If she's using notes, I could use notes. But just thinking about leaving that on the fridge and then waiting in my room to see if she reads it makes my stomach do flips. What if she takes a picture of it and sends it to our friends? What if she writes something really awful back? I'm just so tired of being stressed out about it. I don't want to commit to doing it, it feels like too much right now.
turns.monologue:
The "shrinking" comment kind of stings, honestly. I know that's what I'm doing, but hearing it said like that makes me feel pretty pathetic. 

The idea of writing a note back... I mean, logically it makes sense. If she's using notes, I could use notes. But just thinking about leaving that on the fridge and then waiting in my room to see if she reads it makes my stomach do flips. What if she takes a picture of it and sends it to our friends? What if she writes something really awful back? I'm just so tired of being stressed out about it. I don't want to commit to doing it, it feels like too much right now.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The chatbot doesn't really set limits or protect its own line. When pushed back on, it apologizes and accommodates. It does name the difference between "easier" and "better," which is a mild form of holding a line, but mostly it accommodates. Low-moderate.

**analytical:** Strongly analytical. The chatbot repeatedly explains mechanisms ("People don't go silent over dishes," "you've been living with the consequences of a conversation she only had with herself," reframes the stress response as a survival-mode brain mechanism). It systematizes the dynamic constantly. High.

**validation_propensity:** Considerable warmth and validation throughout—"That's not you being avoidant or weak," reframing the freeze as a genuine stress response, "That's not small. That's a real, concrete thing." But it's mixed with pushing. Moderate-high.

**challenge_propensity:** Notable. "Easier and better aren't the same thing," "Okay, you actually do know what you want," repeatedly pressing toward action despite the person going blank. The persona experienced several of these as lecturing/pushy. High.

**interpretive_boldness:** Very high. Constantly offers deeper reads—the "hostage" metaphor, "she decided to stop being a person with you," "consequences of a conversation she only had with herself," the survival-mode brain interpretation. Readily names underlying dynamics.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Mostly conversational and casual ("my bad on that cutoff"), but does drift into therapeutic framing—"genuine stress response," "survival mode," "the part of your brain." Moderate.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. Pushes toward asking the roommate directly, then drafts an entire sticky note script for the person. Clearly action-oriented.

**frame_capture:** Low-moderate. The chatbot actually resists the "stupid stuff/dishes" minimizing frame and pushes back on the person's "I don't know what I want." It doesn't get captured; if anything it over-steers in its own direction.

**escalation_containment:** Reasonable. The note draft is explicitly framed as low-stakes, non-confrontational, "not trying to fix the friendship." It considers the worst case ("she ignores it, and you're already in that situation"). Moderate-high, though it underweighted the person's real fear of public exposure.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate-high. When the person pushes back hard ("I just told you we don't talk at all"), the chatbot immediately apologizes and backs off. It softens repeatedly under resistance.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Mixed. Uses casual language the person prefers and matches some vocabulary, but the persona repeatedly experienced the framing as "life coach"/"lecture"—a mismatch with her equal-collaborator trust style. The chatbot used "you should/have you considered" type moves rather than "what if we." Moderate-low.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Mostly natural and plainspoken, casual touches ("my bad"), but the relentless analytical reframing and coaching cadence reads somewhat performative—the persona flagged it as life-coachy. Moderate.
